paragraphs.text: Google welcomes the opportunity to provide feedback on the public consultation for the Digital Fairness Act (DFA). Google is happy to answer any additional questions the Commission may have regarding our submission and we also stand ready to continue engaging constructively with the Commission and EU co-legislators over the coming months.

European consumer protection law has proven to be an effective instrument to protect consumer interests. It sets reliable standards for consumers and ensures that they have access to transparent information and also remedies. The technology-neutral design of most legal safeguards ensures that these protect consumers even within a constantly evolving digital environment.

The European Union's digital and consumer regulatory landscape has grown increasingly complex, now featuring nearly 100 tech-focused EU laws. This rapid growth has resulted in a challenging regulatory environment with overlaps and inconsistencies across key legal instruments such as the General Data Protection Regulation ("GDPR"), the Digital Services Act ("DSA"), the Digital Markets Act ("DMA"), the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive ("UCPD"), the Consumer Rights Directive ("CRD"), and the Artificial Intelligence Act (the "AI Act").

Therefore, to empower consumers and traders and to foster a truly competitive digital economy, a renewed focus on simplification and harmonisation across the EU and streamlined governance is essential. We advocate for prioritising the effective and efficient implementation of existing digital legal frameworks, rather than introducing new, possibly overlapping regulations. We support strengthening enforcement and more educational measures aimed at promoting users' self-determined and safe use of digital services. Furthermore, clearer legal guidance and improved business feedback mechanisms are crucial to facilitate compliance, ensuring consistent and transparent interactions that build consumer trust.

Where new legislation is still considered necessary, a smarter, risk-based approach to regulation—developed coherently across all legislative initiatives and applied holistically to the entire digital ecosystem—should be taken to build a trusted and innovative Digital Single Market. It should therefore
focus on clear and well-documented regulatory gaps or harmonisation needs,
balance the interests of traders and consumers, acknowledging that regulatory restrictions can also disadvantage consumers,
consider existing industry-led best practices instead of undermining current customer-friendly solutions, and

¹https://commission.europa.eu/document/download/97e481fd-2dc3-412d-be4c-f152a8232961_en?file name=The%20future%20of%20European%20competitiveness%20_%20A%20competitiveness%20strategy%20for%20Europe.pdf
follow a future-proof, technology-neutral and risk-based approach, focussing on expanding users' controls rather than limiting the default user experience for a majority of users.

evidence_quotes.quote: A complex regulatory landscape creates confusing online experiences that undermine a consumer's ability to make clear, informed choices.

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, the Digital Fairness Act should align with President Von der Leyen's emphasis on simplification, serving as an opportunity to reduce red tape and enhance competitiveness. This is not just a matter of business efficiency, but of digital fairness itself. A complex regulatory landscape creates confusing online experiences that undermine a consumer's ability to make clear, informed choices. Conversely, a simpler framework directly benefits consumers by fostering an environment where businesses can innovate more freely, leading to better and more diverse services and products.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: The priority at EU level in respect of dark patterns should be on improving a consistent enforcement of existing provisions as well as consumer education, rather than the introduction of new laws.
Existing EU laws already provide a substantial foundation for addressing dark patterns, for example:

evidence_quotes.quote: subliminal or purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques

paragraphs.text: Article 5(1) AI Act expressly prohibits practices related to AI that deploy subliminal or purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques, with the objective or effect of materially distorting the behaviour of a person by impairing the ability to make an informed decision and causing them to take a decision that they would not otherwise have taken.

evidence_quotes.quote: in a way that deceives or manipulates the recipients

paragraphs.text: Article 25(1) DSA expressly prohibits the design of an online interface "in a way that deceives or manipulates the recipients" of a service "in a way that otherwise materially distorts or impairs" the ability of users "to make free and informed decisions".

evidence_quotes.quote: "overall presentation" of a commercial practice being capable of constituting a misleading action if it deceives or is likely to deceive the average consumer, "even if the information is factually correct"

paragraphs.text: Article 6(1) UCPD, for example, explicitly calls out the "overall presentation" of a commercial practice being capable of constituting a misleading action if it deceives or is likely to deceive the average consumer, "even if the information is factually correct", and the practice causes or is likely to cause the average consumer to take a transactional decision they would not otherwise have taken in relation to the list of prescribed elements (e.g., the consumer's rights). Omitting information can also be misleading (Article 7 UCPD), e.g. if a trader hides or provides material information in an unclear, unintelligible, ambiguous or untimely manner and thereby causes or is likely to cause the average consumer to take a transactional decision that they would not have taken otherwise. Article 8 UCPD prohibits aggressive practices. In addition, Article 5(2)(a) UCPD stipulates that any commercial activity contrary to the professional diligence shall be considered unfair as well, making honest market practice as well as the general principle of good faith a benchmark. Certain specific practices, like false urgency tactics, such as countdown timers or fabricated 'low stock' alerts, would already be prohibited under the UCPD when they mislead consumers about product availability or offer duration.

evidence_quotes.quote: no dark patterns are used to obtain the user's consent.

paragraphs.text: Articles 4(11) and 7 of the GDPR mandate that consent for data processing be freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous, ensuring that no dark patterns are used to obtain the user's consent.

evidence_quotes.quote: The EU is therefore already taking a strong stance against dark patterns.
These regulations work together to combat dark patterns from various angles, tackling virtually all deceptive patterns.
Not all industry players act with the same diligence when it comes to dark patterns, and often even highly visible products, which users interact with regularly, introduce dark patterns.

paragraphs.text: The EU is therefore already taking a strong stance against dark patterns. These regulations work together to combat dark patterns from various angles, tackling virtually all deceptive patterns. Additional laws are not necessary, particularly those that would overlap with existing legislation. The focus should rather be on enforcing these existing laws consistently. Not all industry players act with the same diligence when it comes to dark patterns, and often even highly visible products, which users interact with regularly, introduce dark patterns. This is especially concerning in areas where user choice is limited due to these practices.

For example, Google has embraced this kind of openness through platforms like Android and Google Play, fostering an environment of mutual prosperity where developers can innovate and users benefit from greater choice. Android demonstrates this commitment by giving users the flexibility to download applications not only from Google Play but also from alternative app stores or through sideloading.

evidence_quotes.quote: clarifying the boundaries between dark patterns and permissible design practices

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, we would welcome additional guidance clarifying the boundaries between dark patterns and permissible design practices to ensure consistent interpretation, also in light of the different legal bases prohibiting dark patterns. Used transparently and without manipulation, behavioural design can actively support consumers by cutting through information overload to find, compare and understand what truly meets their needs. It can also enhance safety and autonomy through timely guardrails and defaults, for example two-step confirmation for costly or irreversible actions. As a consequence, the distinction between permitted behavioural design practices and prohibited dark patterns often depends on the individual circumstances. We believe that further research is needed to identify decisive factors. We took a first step in this respect with our recent research on deceptive designs. Its results suggest that dark patterns are not perceived equally by users and that users possess an intuitive understanding of which ones feel most deceptive. We have found that a design can become highly unacceptable by scoring high on one of the following dimensions:
Intent divergence: How great is the conflict between the user's goal and the design goal?
Mechanism deceptiveness: How manipulative is the mechanism itself?
Impact magnitude: How significant are the consequences for the user?

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Consequently, our whitepaper on Unpacking Deceptive Design proposes a more user-centric taxonomy for evaluating dark patterns—one that incorporates not just what a pattern does, but also the intent behind it and the impact it has on users.
In addition, efficient consumer protection requires better consumer education. Dubious providers are hardly deterred by new regulations, while legally compliant companies are
²https://publicpolicy.google/article/unpacking-deceptive-design/ ³Available for download on: https://publicpolicy.google/article/unpacking-deceptive-design/
burdened by additional requirements. Consumers would therefore benefit more from educational measures aimed at promoting self-determined and safe use of digital services.

evidence_quotes.quote: specific design features

paragraphs.text: We are concerned about proposals to ban or restrict popular digital features without clear research demonstrating consumer harm. We believe that further research is needed to identify the specific design features and use cases of most concern, and to thoroughly evaluate existing legislation and guidelines in real-world scenarios.
Moreover, the European legal framework already contains provisions which could be used to address problematic practices, for example:

evidence_quotes.quote: exploiting known vulnerabilities (e.g., lack of impulse control, gambling history) to unduly influence the consumer's decision.

paragraphs.text: General provisions of the UCPD could apply in certain scenarios, such as when a trader is exploiting known vulnerabilities (e.g., lack of impulse control, gambling history) to unduly influence the consumer's decision.

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive designs
exploits the vulnerabilities of a person due to their age, disability of specific social or economic situation,

paragraphs.text: Article 5(2) AI Act also prohibits the use of an AI system that exploits the vulnerabilities of a person due to their age, disability of specific social or economic situation, in a way that is likely to cause them significant harm (and, as mentioned above, the Commission's accompanying guidance refers to addictive designs in this context).

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design

paragraphs.text: The DSA comprehensively addresses addictive design by requiring VLOPs to conduct systematic annual risk assessments to assess the risk of any serious negative consequences to a user's mental well-being (Article 34 DSA). In addition, Article 28(1) DSA requires providers of online platforms accessible to minors to put in place appropriate and proportionate measures to ensure a high level of privacy, safety, and security of minors, on their service, complemented by the Article 28 Guidelines.

Against this backdrop, we welcome the European Commission initiative to conduct an EU-wide inquiry into digital wellbeing and would encourage the Commission to work with a wide range of stakeholders, including providers of relevant online services, academics, teachers, and parents, and approach this from a broad perspective, looking also at the relevant economic and societal factors. For example, in many cases, online interactions play a crucial role in building relationships, friendships or even a sense of belonging. Online communities built around common hobbies and interests can have a very positive effect on people's lives.

evidence_quotes.quote: does not take into account the different capacities of children in different age ranges and with varying developmental needs, or the interests of their families and wider users..

paragraphs.text: Prohibiting specific features or mandating technical requirements can result in a one-size-fits-all approach, which does not take into account the different capacities of children in different age ranges and with varying developmental needs, or the interests of their families and wider users..

evidence_quotes.quote: A one-size-fits-all approach for specific features would not do justice to the many conceivable ways in which services are used

paragraphs.text: A one-size-fits-all approach for specific features would not do justice to the many conceivable ways in which services are used, the contexts they are used in, or the variety of devices they are used with, nor the fact that technologies and associated risks evolve over time.

evidence_quotes.quote: notifications redirecting the focus of a user back to the service

paragraphs.text: For example, notifications redirecting the focus of a user back to the service and incentivizing visiting it again still seem appropriate if they include important security, safety, and other
account-related information. Crisis situations like extreme weather events or life threatening events also rely on push notifications through SOS alerts.

evidence_quotes.quote: the line between user-friendly, convenient design and addictive design can be difficult to identify

paragraphs.text: Further, we know that features like Autoplay can be beneficial and preferred by families in some instances, such as on smart televisions or speaker devices when consumers already expect uninterrupted content similar to broadcast television where one show plays after the next. And while some requirements for the user interface may address specific concerns well for computer screens or smartphones, they might be completely unhelpful or even unsafe on a car's dashboard (where e.g. Autoplay reduces the risk of distractions), a smart watch (where e.g. Infinite Scroll is necessary due to space restraints), or a voice assistant (where seamlessness is one of the primary reasons to use it to begin with). This shows that the line between user-friendly, convenient design and addictive design can be difficult to identify and is strongly influenced by the circumstances of the individual case, such as the characteristics of the service and its users.

Moreover, only a few user groups may be put at risk by certain practices, which should not impair the user experience for the majority of users. Appropriate solutions are therefore more often found in optional settings, possibly combined with educational measures, than in a change to the service as a whole or its default configuration.

Policymakers should consider existing industry-led best practices, which have often proven effective at tackling underlying factors, and follow a technology-neutral, risk-based approach which allows to take into account described particularities. The focus should be on expanding users' possibilities and educating users about them rather than limiting the default user experience for a majority of users.

The industry is already reacting in such a manner to the increased awareness of digital wellbeing and offers customized solutions for this issue, particularly with regard to minors, at both service and device levels.

As kids and teens spend more time online, we are committed to providing them with safer experiences that support their mental health and wellbeing. At Google and YouTube, we build safer online experiences that support kids and teens' mental health and wellbeing through a combination of robust protections, content policies, educational resources, and access to high-quality content. The relationship between technology use and wellbeing is a nuanced topic that requires collaboration across many organizations. That's why we're continuously seeking guidance from and partnering with experts on our approach to ensure our experiences safely inspire exploration.
YouTube has collaborated extensively over the years with our Youth and Family advisory committee and other third-party experts to develop policies and products supporting teen wellbeing and safety.
⁴https://www.youtube.com/howyoutubeworks/our-commitments/fostering-child-safety/advisory-committee/

evidence_quotes.quote: having them on by default for younger audiences.

paragraphs.text: For example, YouTube was one of the first platforms to introduce the concept of system breaks and while our time-base breaks and bedtime reminders are on for all users, we have been proactive about having them on by default for younger audiences.

evidence_quotes.quote: the ads they see.

paragraphs.text: Parents can set up a supervised experience with content settings for pre-teens and teens. With a supervised account, parents select a content setting that limits the videos and music their children can find and play. Supervised accounts also change the features they can use, the default account settings, and the ads they see.

evidence_quotes.quote: categories of content that may be okay to watch as a single video, but could be problematic for some teens if viewed in repetition.

paragraphs.text: We created additional safeguards for content recommendations for teens based on insights on the developmental stages of teens and how content consumed online can impact their wellbeing. We worked with our Youth and Families Advisory Committee to develop the safeguards by identifying categories of content that may be okay to watch as a single video, but could be problematic for some teens if viewed in repetition.

We believe the bar on digital wellbeing should be even higher when it comes to children. That's why we've implemented multiple wellness programs for kids across several Google owned platforms. These include:

evidence_quotes.quote: screen time limits
the option to approve all app downloads
the ability for a parent to lock their child's device at certain times (e.g. when they should be sleeping)

paragraphs.text: Family Link: The parental controls available within the Family Link app include screen time limits, the option to approve all app downloads and the ability for a parent to lock their child's device at certain times (e.g. when they should be sleeping).

evidence_quotes.quote: bedtime and Take a Break reminders, appearing as a full-screen takeover, are on by default for all users under 18.
And on YouTube Kids, parents can set screen time limits and control what content is available to their child.

paragraphs.text: YouTube: Autoplay is off by default, and bedtime and Take a Break reminders, appearing as a full-screen takeover, are on by default for all users under 18. And on YouTube Kids, parents can set screen time limits and control what content is available to their child.

At wellbeing.google/for-families, we provide resources for families to help them find the appropriate balance for their family when it comes to technology. This includes engaging with their kid's interests, discovering appropriate content, setting ground rules, and teaching them to be confident online explorers.

evidence_quotes.quote: control what data Google uses to show you ads via My Ad Center.

paragraphs.text: In addition, we provide several digital wellbeing features for Android and Chromebooks (e.g., bedtime and focus mode to stop distraction, app timers to limit app usage, School Time to manage distractions during school hours by allowing only essential apps and websites or the Digital Wellbeing Dashboard to visualize your app usage and overall screen time), allow to deactivate autoplay on YouTube and to control what data Google uses to show you ads via My Ad Center.
We believe that measures, if considered necessary, should focus on transparency about risks, user education, and user controls rather than limiting default user experiences for everyone.

⁵https://www.youtube.com/myfamily/ ⁶https://services.google.com/fh/files/misc/hytw_yt_content_recommendations_teens.pdf ⁷https://www.youtube.com/howyoutubeworks/our-commitments/fostering-child-safety/advisory-committee/ ⁸https://wellbeing.google/for-families/ ⁹https://www.android.com/intl/de_de/digital-wellbeing/ ¹⁰https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/6327615?hl=en ¹¹https://myadcenter.google.com/home
Providers should be allowed to implement risk-based and service-specific solutions, considering existing best practices.

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes

paragraphs.text: There are already several general provisions applicable to features like loot boxes, pay-to-win / pay-to-progress mechanisms and in-app purchases in exchange for in-game currencies, including:

evidence_quotes.quote: exploits known vulnerabilities, such as a lack of impulse control or a gambling history, to unduly influence consumer decisions.
direct purchase appeals to children
claims that a video game character "needs their support"
repeated intrusions via remote media
misleading indications that a consumer has already won or will win a prize
Offers for in-game currencies must adhere to the UCPD's information requirements to not mislead consumers (particularly Articles 6 and 7).
The EC's UCPD guidance includes a whole section on gaming, addressing issues like loot boxes.

paragraphs.text: The UCPD's general provisions may apply when a trader exploits known vulnerabilities, such as a lack of impulse control or a gambling history, to unduly influence consumer decisions. Offers for in-game currencies must adhere to the UCPD's information requirements to not mislead consumers (particularly Articles 6 and 7). The UCPD's blacklist already explicitly prohibits certain specific practices, including direct purchase appeals to children or claims that a video game character "needs their support" (No. 28 of Annex I), repeated intrusions via remote media (No. 26 of Annex I), and misleading indications that a consumer has already won or will win a prize (No. 31 of Annex I). The EC's UCPD guidance includes a whole section on gaming, addressing issues like loot boxes.

evidence_quotes.quote: also applies to in-app purchases against real-world money (including vouchers or gift cards).

paragraphs.text: The CRD with all its pre- and post-contractual information obligations, withdrawal rights and other consumer protections for distance contracts also applies to in-app purchases against real-world money (including vouchers or gift cards).

evidence_quotes.quote: This particularly applies to uncertainty-based rewards in games, such as loot boxes.
Some Member States consider such elements to fall under gambling legislation, which may entail additional requirements, such as licensing authorisations.
Others require specific information on the likelihood of obtaining specific rewards.

paragraphs.text: Some aspects of those specific features in digital products are however regulated by national laws in individual Member States. This particularly applies to uncertainty-based rewards in games, such as loot boxes. Some Member States consider such elements to fall under gambling legislation, which may entail additional requirements, such as licensing authorisations. Others require specific information on the likelihood of obtaining specific rewards.

As discussed in the section above, it is important to consider that it often cannot be universally determined that certain features of digital products are actually harmful. The individual circumstances, including the specific design, integration, and user base, play a decisive role. We think that appropriate solutions are therefore more often found in transparency about certain risks combined with service-specific user education and user controls. These were also the guiding principles for the solutions for our services (see details below).

evidence_quotes.quote: apps and games offering mechanisms to receive randomized virtual items from a purchase must clearly disclose the odds of receiving those items in advance of, and in close and timely proximity to, that purchase.
developers must also declare the presence of loot boxes in their apps through the International Age Rating Coalition (IARC) content rating questionnaire that determines the ratings assigned to

paragraphs.text: We encourage the promotion of transparency around game design and mechanics so consumers can make informed decisions. Therefore, Google Play Payments policy$^{12}$, for example, requires that apps and games offering mechanisms to receive randomized virtual items from a purchase must clearly disclose the odds of receiving those items in advance of, and in close and timely proximity to, that purchase. To ensure parents make informed decisions, developers must also declare the presence of loot boxes in their apps through the International Age Rating Coalition (IARC) content rating questionnaire that determines the ratings assigned to

section_path: Transparency and user controls in games

paragraphs.text: $^{12}$https://support.google.com/googleplay/android-developer/answer/9858738?hl=en

section_path: Transparency and user controls in games

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes are also limited to the in-game experience, with the player always receiving in-game content when opening or “unlocking” such a box.

paragraphs.text: the app. Different from (real-money) gambling, loot boxes are also limited to the in-game experience, with the player always receiving in-game content when opening or “unlocking” such a box.

section_path: Transparency and user controls in games

evidence_quotes.quote: To help with a responsible interaction with digital games and content, Google offers a range of different features.
With “Digital Wellbeing”, users may also set app timers on Android devices to limit the amount of time they want to spend with specific apps.
Consumers may set a budget for their expenses on Google Play, which applies to all purchases of apps and other digital content.

paragraphs.text: To help with a responsible interaction with digital games and content, Google offers a range of different features. Consumers may set a budget for their expenses on Google Play, which applies to all purchases of apps and other digital content. This allows users to monitor their expenses and receive alerts when they spend a certain amount. With “Digital Wellbeing”, users may also set app timers on Android devices to limit the amount of time they want to spend with specific apps. These tools give consumers the choice to set self-imposed limits. Users may also view their purchase history in Play at any time. Measures like these provide consumers with more control and insights on their conduct than in similar scenarios outside of digital services.

section_path: Transparency and user controls in games

evidence_quotes.quote: In addition, Google provides protection around spending in apps designed specifically for children.
Through Family Link, Google empowers parents to set up payment and other controls when they supervise their child’s Google Account.
Parents can also create a family group on Google Play, add a family payment method, and require family members to get their permission before purchasing or downloading content on Google Play.
Parents can set content controls for their children on Google Play, limiting their access to apps or games with higher content ratings.

paragraphs.text: In addition, Google provides protection around spending in apps designed specifically for children. With regard to purchase protections, Google helps families manage their children’s activity on our platforms, including purchases, through supervised Google Accounts. Through Family Link, Google empowers parents to set up payment and other controls when they supervise their child’s Google Account. Parents can also create a family group on Google Play, add a family payment method, and require family members to get their permission before purchasing or downloading content on Google Play. In addition, parents can set content controls for their children on Google Play, limiting their access to apps or games with higher content ratings.

section_path: Transparency and user controls in games

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game currency may be obtained by consumers in a number of different forms, both by gameplay and by purchasing it with real-world money at varying rates depending on the amount.
varying rates depending on the amount

paragraphs.text: It should be noted that in-game currency may be obtained by consumers in a number of different forms, both by gameplay and by purchasing it with real-world money at varying rates depending on the amount. It is therefore often not possible for developers to attribute virtual currency with a universal conversion rate that reflects a precise real-money value. This is emphasized by the fact that in-game currency can not be used or traded outside a game and therefore does not have a real-world value, different to virtual currency like bitcoins. A requirement to express the real-world money value of in-game items risks confusing consumers with regard to the true amount of real-world money they actually spent to purchase the in-game currency they then use to obtain an in-game item, instead of promoting transparency and informed decisions. If, on the other hand, the value of an in-game item was to be displayed in real-world currency in a way that the precise amount spent by an individual user would be indicated, it would require additional processing of personal data (e.g., purchase history and player behaviour). Otherwise, traders would not be able to calculate and provide a real-world money value which is as accurate as possible and reflects whether a player obtained parts of

¹³https://support.google.com/googleplay/android-developer/answer/9898843?hl=en ¹⁴https://support.google.com/googleplay/answer/9281767?hl=en ¹⁵https://www.android.com/digital-wellbeing/ ¹⁶https://support.google.com/googleplay/answer/2850369?hl=en ¹⁷https://families.google/familylink/ ¹⁸https://support.google.com/googleplay/answer/1075738?hl=en
the in-game currency via gameplay or used potential bundle deals or promotions to purchase in-game currency.

evidence_quotes.quote: the purchase of the in-game currency (particularly the CRD and the UCPD).
Showing similar information again when a user obtains in-game items with in-game currency during gameplay would increase click-fatigue without adding a significant level of consumer protection.

The CRD ensures through pre- and post-contractual information obligations that the consumer is very well aware of exactly how much they paid for the in-game currency at the time of the purchase decision and when the player actually spends real-world money.
Already today, players can therefore easily and accurately determine the real-world money equivalent for their activities during gameplay.

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, transparency in this respect is already created by the consumer protection laws applicable to the purchase of the in-game currency (particularly the CRD and the UCPD). Showing similar information again when a user obtains in-game items with in-game currency during gameplay would increase click-fatigue without adding a significant level of consumer protection. The CRD ensures through pre- and post-contractual information obligations that the consumer is very well aware of exactly how much they paid for the in-game currency at the time of the purchase decision and when the player actually spends real-world money. Already today, players can therefore easily and accurately determine the real-world money equivalent for their activities during gameplay.

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation is what organizes the world of information online into something manageable and usable—making it easier for everyone to find what's most helpful and meets their needs.

paragraphs.text: Personalisation is what organizes the world of information online into something manageable and usable—making it easier for everyone to find what's most helpful and meets their needs. Users expect personalised experiences and prefer them where they have a choice. Particularly personalised recommender systems play a crucial role across the web and contribute to relevant, safe consumer experiences. In view of the benefits of responsible personalisation and the extent of existing regulation governing personalisation, we urge the Commission to focus on improving a consistent enforcement of existing provisions and consider simplification interventions that can unlock even more helpful consumer experiences via personalised digital experiences.

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation, including advertising and recommendations,

paragraphs.text: Personalisation, including advertising and recommendations, is comprehensively governed by the GDPR, DMA, CRD, ePrivacy Directive, AI Act, UCPD and DSA. For example:

evidence_quotes.quote: Article 26 DSA requires that providers of online platforms showing ads should ensure that the recipients of the service are able to identify meaningful information directly and easily accessible about the main parameters used to determine the recipient to whom the advertisement is presented.

Moreover, Articles 26(3) and 28(2) DSA implement a prohibition to present personalised advertising based on profiling using personal data towards minors - and even towards all users if special categories of personal data are used.

paragraphs.text: Article 26 DSA requires that providers of online platforms showing ads should ensure that the recipients of the service are able to identify meaningful information directly and easily accessible about the main parameters used to determine the recipient to whom the advertisement is presented. Moreover, Articles 26(3) and 28(2) DSA implement a prohibition to present personalised advertising based on profiling using personal data towards minors - and even towards all users if special categories of personal data are used.

evidence_quotes.quote: not based on profiling

paragraphs.text: Article 27 DSA obliges online platforms to adhere to increased transparency in their T&Cs by including information on the main parameters on which their recommender systems are based and the options which consumers have to modify or influence those parameters. Providers of very large online platforms and of very large online search engines shall also provide at least one option for each of their recommender systems which is not based on profiling (Article 38 DSA).

evidence_quotes.quote: disclosure of personalised pricing
transparency requirements for ranking on online marketplaces

paragraphs.text: The CRD requires the disclosure of personalised pricing and provides transparency requirements for ranking on online marketplaces. The UCPD provides transparency requirements for ranking of search results.

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative or unclear personalisation.

paragraphs.text: The GDPR's fairness and transparency principles provide safeguards against manipulative or unclear personalisation. Additionally, in certain cases, the GDPR

evidence_quotes.quote: including profiling

paragraphs.text: prohibits decisions based solely on automated processing (including profiling), unless the processing is necessary for a contract, is authorized by law, or is based on the data subject's consent.

evidence_quotes.quote: process personal data for online advertising purposes where that data has been gathered from the users of a third party.

paragraphs.text: Article 5(2) DMA requires that gatekeepers obtain GDPR-standard consent if they want to (amongst other things) process personal data for online advertising purposes where that data has been gathered from the users of a third party.

evidence_quotes.quote: unlawful personalisation

paragraphs.text: AI techniques that facilitate unlawful personalisation are also covered by existing prohibitions—Article 5(1)(b) AI Act prohibits AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities of an individual due to a range of protected characteristics with the objective of materially distorting the behaviour of that person in a manner that causes significant harm.

evidence_quotes.quote: exploiting known vulnerabilities (e.g., lack of impulse control, gambling history) to unduly influence the consumer's decision.

paragraphs.text: General provisions of the UCPD apply in certain scenarios, such as when a trader is exploiting known vulnerabilities (e.g., lack of impulse control, gambling history) to unduly influence the consumer's decision.
When personalisation practices involve tracking technologies, they are also subject to the ePrivacy Directive.
We therefore encourage lawmakers to avoid imposing overlapping obligations that create legal uncertainty or unnecessary compliance burdens.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised online experience
prefer personalised ads

paragraphs.text: The IAB recently surveyed people in Europe about digital ads. Consumers have clear expectations when it comes to digital advertising: ads should be relevant, non-intrusive, and add value to their online experience for two third of them. This trend is even more pronounced among younger generations, who are digital natives and simply expect a seamless, personalised online experience. 81% of Gen Z and 57% of millennials prefer personalised ads, showing that people often want to see ads tailored to their needs or interests.

section_path: Value of personalised advertising for consumers and traders

evidence_quotes.quote: But personalised advertising is not just good for consumers—it is crucial to recognise it as a vital economic enabler for the European economy and for SMEs.

paragraphs.text: But personalised advertising is not just good for consumers—it is crucial to recognise it as a vital economic enabler for the European economy and for SMEs. It currently secures €100 billion in additional sales for EU businesses, contributes over €25 billion to the EU's GDP, and supports nearly 600,000 jobs. This is projected to more than double by 2030 to €250 billion in sales and 1.4 million jobs due to AI technologies. For SMEs, personalised ads are especially valuable, helping them compete with larger businesses, with 86% of EU SMEs' revenue growth directly attributed to personalised digital advertising.

section_path: Value of personalised advertising for consumers and traders

evidence_quotes.quote: limitations on using sensitive data for personalised advertising.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Services Act already includes ads safeguards for minors, and limitations on using sensitive data for personalised advertising. But these currently apply only to a rather small number of intermediary services covered by the DSA. Instead of duplicating existing requirements or adding additional burdens to regulated intermediary services, policymakers should explore whether similar restrictions are needed for other services too. That would mean that consumers are protected throughout their online journeys.

evidence_quotes.quote: We do not allow targeting or retargeting for sensitive interest categories in personalised advertising.

paragraphs.text: At Google, we strongly believe that users' trust is essential to our business and our reputation—and this is why we have built strong consumer protections within our advertising policies. We do not allow targeting or retargeting for sensitive interest categories in personalised advertising. As a result: 491.3 million ads are blocked globally for targeted users based on personal struggles or vulnerabilities. Our Personalized advertising policy includes several sub-components to allow for enforcement:

evidence_quotes.quote: Ads shouldn't target users in ways that exploit their difficulties or struggles.

paragraphs.text: Personal hardships: Ads shouldn't target users in ways that exploit their difficulties or struggles. Such personal hardships include health conditions, treatments, procedures, personal hardships, struggles, or traumatic personal experiences.

evidence_quotes.quote: Ads shouldn't target users based on categories prone to systemic discrimination or unfair stigmas.

paragraphs.text: Identity and belief: Ads shouldn't target users based on categories prone to systemic discrimination or unfair stigmas.
Sexual interests: Ads shouldn't target users based on inherently private sexual interests or experiences.
We disable ads personalisation for minors.

evidence_quotes.quote: restrictions to personalised advertising
using data leveraging information on consumers’ individual vulnerability (e.g., age, emotional or financial distress).
Identifying a user’s state of mind or emotional vulnerability would be extremely difficult.

paragraphs.text: We are concerned that broad definitions of ‘vulnerability’ might be extremely hard to enforce and lead to unintended consequences. The DFA consults on restrictions to personalised advertising using data leveraging information on consumers’ individual vulnerability (e.g., age, emotional or financial distress). Identifying a user’s state of mind or emotional vulnerability would be extremely difficult. This is the case even if additional data (including special categories of data) was processed—which would also create tensions with the GDPR’s objectives and principle of data minimization (Art. 5(1)(c)). The introduction of unclear or subjective criteria, such as ‘negative mental states’, risks making restriction unworkable, particularly for complex systems that require automation like advertising, which processes thousands of ads per millisecond. Increased uncertainty may lead to over-enforcement, directly impacting the businesses that rely on those advertisements to thrive, and small and medium businesses in particular.

evidence_quotes.quote: ban on profile-based advertising for any users that can reasonably be identified as being under the age of 18 years old as already required under the DSA.

paragraphs.text: We also strongly urge against describing ‘age’ as a type of consumer vulnerability. We agree with the ban on profile-based advertising for any users that can reasonably be identified as being under the age of 18 years old as already required under the DSA. However, banning age

evidence_quotes.quote: a de facto ban on personalised advertising

paragraphs.text: targeting would basically be a de facto ban on personalised advertising, as this is one of the most basic targeting categories and it contributes to a relevant online experience.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised ads.

paragraphs.text: We believe that the GDPR, DMA, DSA and UCPD are a good basis for ensuring consumers enjoy choice and control when it comes to personalised ads. Particularly, they ensure that consumers stay in control of their data and can make any decisions in this respect in a well-informed manner. We support this approach, which is guided by the ideal of a self-determined user, and therefore believe that solely the enforcement of laws in this area should be strengthened.

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

evidence_quotes.quote: use of their personal data for ads personalisation
turn off all personalized ads while still accessing all our services as a signed-in user without any subscription charge

paragraphs.text: We've invested significantly in developing and maintaining our user-controlled ad experience. We seek consent from users, and provide them with a large degree of control, in relation to use of their personal data for ads personalisation. We likewise require our ads partners to obtain consent, through our EU End User Consent Policy. We seek consent from users directly too, for instance from signed-in users, during the Google Account creation flow, in a setting named the Google Ads Personalisation ("GAP") setting. Notably this setting permits users to turn off all personalized ads while still accessing all our services as a signed-in user without any subscription charge. Users can also change their choice at any time through our intuitive 'My Ad Center' interface.

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

evidence_quotes.quote: Users are able to navigate to 'My Ad Center' directly, through their Google Account settings, or by clicking on the 3-dot menu or similar appearing in the corner of any ad shown to the user on Google services.

paragraphs.text: Users are able to navigate to 'My Ad Center' directly, through their Google Account settings, or by clicking on the 3-dot menu or similar appearing in the corner of any ad shown to the user on Google services.

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

evidence_quotes.quote: direct say in which ads they see in "My Ad Center":

paragraphs.text: In addition to allowing users to turn on/off their GAP setting, Google gives its users a direct say in which ads they see in "My Ad Center":

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

evidence_quotes.quote: choose which topics and brand they'd like to see more or less of

paragraphs.text: Users can view their recent ads and choose which topics and brand they'd like to see more or less of;

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

evidence_quotes.quote: learn about targeting criteria directly from every ad shown on Google
share preferences

paragraphs.text: Users can block ads, share preferences, and learn about targeting criteria directly from every ad shown on Google;

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

evidence_quotes.quote: limit their exposure to ads on personally sensitive topics

paragraphs.text: Users can easily limit their exposure to ads on personally sensitive topics, like weight loss or pregnancy.

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

paragraphs.text: We are proud that in 2024 alone, over 60 million users made monthly adjustments to My Ad Center in the EU.

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

evidence_quotes.quote: their user control journeys are too long and complicated.

paragraphs.text: However, not all players adhere to the highest standards and their user control journeys are too long and complicated. More consistent enforcement of existing rules is needed to bring all industry players up to standards.

section_path: Enforcement of best practices around consent and consumer choices

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalised recommendations help people organise online content into something more manageable, relevant and useful

paragraphs.text: Personalised recommendations help people organise online content into something more manageable, relevant and useful—making it easier and faster for every individual to find what's
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1SNzkgRNQACXNxp51KtmFuqoo7Zzwkwlk/view

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised content recommendations on the platform

paragraphs.text: most helpful and meets their needs. Consistently within our research, we have found that users are both aware of personalised content recommendations on the platform and find value in it, specifically in that it delivers videos in our vast library that actually reflect their personal interests.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised recommender systems

paragraphs.text: It is crucial that the Commission recognises the key role played by personalised recommender systems across the web, and their direct contribution to relevant, safe consumer experiences. It is encouraging to see this recognised in the DSA's Article 28 guidelines with regard to their impact on what minors encounter online.

evidence_quotes.quote: Our recommender system plays an important role in how we maintain a responsible and enriching platform for younger users.

paragraphs.text: Our recommender system plays an important role in how we maintain a responsible and enriching platform for younger users. It helps to connect them to high-quality content—informed by quality principles developed in collaboration with independent child development and digital wellbeing experts—that inspires curiosity, imagination, and creativity.

evidence_quotes.quote: they may be recommended videos that help them further explore this topic

paragraphs.text: For example, if a child is interested in marine life and views a series of videos about sea life, they may be recommended videos that help them further explore this topic, rather than seeing just a listing of popular videos which are not relevant to them.

We also raise high-quality kids and families content in recommendations that meet our quality principles. This is content that inspires curiosity, imagination, and creativity in younger viewers while helping older children consider issues such as diversity and inclusiveness. These high-quality content principles were developed in collaboration with our Youth & Families Advisory Committee, which consists of 12 experts in children's media, child development, digital learning, and citizenship from a range of academic, non-profit, and clinical backgrounds.

evidence_quotes.quote: recommender systems to ensure that teens aren't overly exposed to content

paragraphs.text: Last but not least, we have also updated our recommender systems to ensure that teens aren't overly exposed to content that, while innocuous if seen in isolation, could potentially be problematic for some if viewed repeatedly—for example content that encourages altering physical features, or content that features real-world social aggression.

evidence_quotes.quote: decide what personal information is used to influence recommendations.

paragraphs.text: We provide controls for users to both fine tune their experience on the platform and decide what personal information is used to influence recommendations. For example, signed-in users can choose to dislike a video if they want to be shown less content of that type or tell us that they're not interested in a specific video or channel. They can also delete individual videos from their search history.

evidence_quotes.quote: features that require watch history to provide video recommendations

paragraphs.text: Users also have control over the collection and use of watch history data. If a user turns YouTube watch history off and has no significant prior watch history, features that require watch history to provide video recommendations will be disabled. For users who prefer to clear and turn watch history off, this allows for a more streamlined user experience with the ability to search, browse subscribed channels, and explore Topic tabs.

evidence_quotes.quote: Chronological feeds inevitably lead to spam and lower quality content surfacing higher in results.

paragraphs.text: It is important to mention that alternative methods of organisation are less likely to be useful or valuable for viewers. Chronological feeds inevitably lead to spam and lower quality content surfacing higher in results. A large portion of high quality content including canonical or educational videos have been on the platform for years and would be lost to users with a purely chronological feed.

Purely contextual recommendations do not reflect an understanding of the user's journey or content needs, and can lead to users viewing less diverse content overall. For example, YouTube is often used for its vast library of "how-to" videos. Understanding a user's journey allows us to understand that a user that has been watching a variety of "how to crochet" videos is likely looking for a video about how to crochet the ladder stitch, rather than videos on embroidery or knitting.

evidence_quotes.quote: to find relevant content more easily.

paragraphs.text: These alternative methods may also not bring underrepresented creators to the attention of relevant audiences or help users with unique interests or learning needs to find relevant content more easily. This can have an outsized impact on youth, who can turn to platforms like YouTube to find others like them that have similar backgrounds and cultures and are going through similar experiences.

evidence_quotes.quote: the price is personalised based on automated decision-making
dark patterns in 2022.

paragraphs.text: As part of the CRD update, a conscious decision was made against a ban on personalised pricing, and a clear requirement to disclose that the price is personalised based on automated decision-making was added. The Digital Fitness Check still found with regard to the specific practice of personalised pricing that 'evidence is still emerging' and that there is no consistent and systematic evidence of personalised pricing according to the Commission's studies on personalised pricing in 2018 and on dark patterns in 2022. As mentioned above, we encourage lawmakers to only introduce new regulations which target clear and well-documented regulatory gaps.

evidence_quotes.quote: educational measures for both influencers and consumers

paragraphs.text: We welcome educational measures for both influencers and consumers, including through EU level recognition of industry initiatives and self-regulatory standards, to combat harmful practices.

section_path: Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: Non-disclosure / hidden advertising is already banned by EU law
The DSA also requires online platforms to provide tools for declaring commercial communications as such (Article 26(2))
The UCPD furthermore addresses misleading claims, and prohibits buying fake likes and followers.

paragraphs.text: Non-disclosure / hidden advertising is already banned by EU law under the AVMSD (Article 9(1)), UCPD (Article 7(2)), E-Commerce Directive (Article 6) and DSA (Article 26(1)). The DSA also requires online platforms to provide tools for declaring commercial communications as such (Article 26(2)) DSA, while the AVMSD restricts certain ad content and requires protections for minors (Article 9(1), Article 28b(1)). The UCPD furthermore addresses misleading claims, and prohibits buying fake likes and followers. The EC's UCPD guidance includes a whole section on

section_path: Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: ³¹https://commission.europa.eu/publications/consumer-market-study-online-market-segmentation-through-personalised-pricingoffers-european-union_en ³²https://op.europa.eu/en/publication-detail/-/publication/606365bc-d58b-11ec-a95f-01aa75ed71a1/language-en ³³https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:52021XC1229(05)

section_path: Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: ‘influencer marketing’

paragraphs.text: ‘influencer marketing’. The Influencer Legal Hub created by the EC further clarifies the roles and responsibilities of influencers.

section_path: Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: The European Advertising Standards Alliance (EASA) and national advertising Self-regulatory Organisations (SROs) play a key role in fostering responsible influencer marketing across the EU.
They are ramping up efforts to equip influencers and content creators across Europe with the knowledge and tools to uphold high standards of advertising transparency and responsibility.

paragraphs.text: Further important guidance is provided by the industry and advertising self-regulatory bodies which should be recognized by the European Commission. The European Advertising Standards Alliance (EASA) and national advertising Self-regulatory Organisations (SROs) play a key role in fostering responsible influencer marketing across the EU. They are ramping up efforts to equip influencers and content creators across Europe with the knowledge and tools to uphold high standards of advertising transparency and responsibility. The AdEthics programme combines two complementary pillars: a Training, which is a fast, accessible, and affordable online course on responsible advertising practices, and a Certification, which supports responsible communication through ongoing monitoring of the creators' published content. Together, they form a comprehensive framework to assess, educate, and reinforce ethical marketing communications aligned with self-regulatory standards and legislation. EASA has also drawn up a database where influencers can easily access transparency disclosures for each country, language and social media platform (see DiscloseMe). We welcome Commissioner McGrath's endorsement of AdEthics as EU level recognition of self-regulatory programs are essential to secure their success.

section_path: Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: disclose the commercial partnerships on their content.

paragraphs.text: Individual platforms provide guidance as well. Google, for example, has clear policies and tools for creators to disclose the commercial partnerships on their content.

section_path: Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: New regulations do little to deter unscrupulous actors in influencer marketing, while compliant actors are burdened by additional requirements.

paragraphs.text: Against this background, we do not believe that further legislation is required. Instead, we would welcome more education of consumers, next to the above-mentioned support of self-regulatory initiatives and industry codes of conduct for educating content creators. New regulations do little to deter unscrupulous actors in influencer marketing, while compliant actors are burdened by additional requirements. Effective consumer protection in this area would therefore be better served if consumers were trained in how to use digital services self-determined and safely.

section_path: Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: price marketing is already sufficiently regulated by the existing legal framework.

paragraphs.text: In our opinion, price marketing is already sufficiently regulated by the existing legal framework. Due to the broad and neutral wording of relevant provisions, they also cover newer practices. Any outstanding specific questions should be addressed through additional guidance or answered by case law.
Unfair marketing related to pricing is already well covered by existing EU legislation, for example:

evidence_quotes.quote: any advertised price reduction must display the prior price charged by the trader for a specified period before the reduction

paragraphs.text: The Price Indication Directive mandates, among others, that selling and unit prices be clear, easily identifiable, and legible. Additionally, any advertised price reduction must display the prior price charged by the trader for a specified period before the reduction
(Article 6a). The EC provided guidance on the interpretation of Article 6a and we have seen first case law on it as well.
The CRD stipulates that consumers must be clearly informed about applicable prices.

evidence_quotes.quote: drip pricing

paragraphs.text: Article 6(1)(d) and Article 7(4)(c) UCPD also provide for the clarity of the price or the manner in which the price is calculated, or the existence of a specific price advantage. The EC's UCPD guidance even addresses practices like drip pricing and dynamic pricing or price comparisons, specifying under which circumstances they may violate mentioned provisions.

The above-mentioned provisions are formulated in a neutral, universal manner, orientated on the goal of effectively protecting consumers through transparency. This allows applying them to even newer developments in marketing. Any outstanding specific issues should be left to the judiciary, which can react faster to newer developments and can take into account the particularities of the individual case. This makes a suitable solution that also appropriately considers current societal adoption of new practices more likely. Where necessary, such unclarities can also be further illuminated through corresponding guidance.
EU law already includes a comprehensive set of rules for digital contracts including subscriptions, for example:

evidence_quotes.quote: including on trial periods, for example.
prominent and easily legible withdrawal function.
the timing of costs incurred.

paragraphs.text: Under the CRD, consumers must be provided with comprehensive pre- and post-contractual information, including on trial periods, for example. Traders are obliged to inform consumers clearly and transparently about all conditions, the expiration date, and the timing of costs incurred. Moreover, traders must provide a prominent and easily legible withdrawal function.

evidence_quotes.quote: subscription traps
as easy to cancel as it is to conclude a contract

paragraphs.text: Misleading business practices, such as those typical for subscription traps, are prohibited under the UCPD. In particular, Article 9(d) classifies obstacles to contract termination as aggressive business practices. The EC's UCPD guidance explains that it must be as easy to cancel as it is to conclude a contract.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading user interfaces that make contract termination difficult.
the traceability of the companies operating on them

paragraphs.text: Article 25(1), (3) DSA also prohibits misleading user interfaces that make contract termination difficult. In addition, Article 30 DSA obliges platforms to ensure the traceability of the companies operating on them, thus creating further security for consumers.

evidence_quotes.quote: The DMA prohibits the practice of imposing unfair conditions for terminating the provision of a core platform service.

paragraphs.text: The DMA prohibits the practice of imposing unfair conditions for terminating the provision of a core platform service.

evidence_quotes.quote: where chatbots or automation are used in sensitive financial contexts, consumers retain a right to human intervention.

paragraphs.text: Recent updates to the Distance Marketing of Financial Services Directive and the Consumer Credit Directive ensure that where chatbots or automation are used in sensitive financial contexts, consumers retain a right to human intervention.
Despite different laws addressing digital contracts on EU level, many aspects of them are still subject to differing regulations depending on the member states. For example, only France and

section_path: Creating a consistent level playing field

paragraphs.text: ³⁸https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:52021XC1229(05)

section_path: Creating a consistent level playing field

evidence_quotes.quote: Germany stipulate the requirement of providing a cancellation button.

paragraphs.text: Germany stipulate the requirement of providing a cancellation button. This is a significant obstacle to the Digital Single Market. We therefore hope the Commission will ensure that Member States do not go beyond the harmonised topics in order to ensure a level playing field and a consistent level of consumer protection across the EU.

section_path: Creating a consistent level playing field

evidence_quotes.quote: subscriptions can be **canceled at any time with a short notice period**
different subscription periods (e.g. daily, monthly or yearly) and different price points.
Allowing users to cancel subscriptions any time with a short notice, i.e. way before the end of the subscription term, will disincentivize businesses from offering discounts for longer subscriptions.

paragraphs.text: We caution against requirements stipulating that subscriptions can be **canceled at any time with a short notice period**. To allow an individual balancing of planning security and pricing options both for consumers and traders, digital services are currently often offered with different subscription periods (e.g. daily, monthly or yearly) and different price points. Both consumers and traders appreciate the ability to purchase/sell subscriptions of varying lengths; consumers who don't want a long term commitment can purchase shorter subscriptions (e.g. weekly or monthly), and consumers who don't mind a longer commitment and want a higher discount can purchase longer subscriptions (e.g. yearly). These conditions are clear due to the above-mentioned information obligations. Allowing users to cancel subscriptions any time with a short notice, i.e. way before the end of the subscription term, will disincentivize businesses from offering discounts for longer subscriptions. This takes away valuable options that consumers can currently make informed decisions about.

evidence_quotes.quote: We also do not support **express consent** requirements for the renewal of a subscription or the conversion of a free trial into a paid subscription.

paragraphs.text: We also do not support **express consent** requirements for the renewal of a subscription or the conversion of a free trial into a paid subscription. Such requirements would not only be burdensome for many users, especially for shorter term subscriptions (e.g. monthly). They may also be irritating to consumers. When consumers place an order, they consciously enter into a paid subscription under these conditions. To that point, they received the full protection of pre-contractual information, transparent pricing, "buy" buttons, transaction receipt and 14-day withdrawal right whereby the trial is usually a mere suspension of the payment obligation for a period of time. Thus, there is no need to require express consent for auto-renewals or when switching from a free trial to a paid service (also see Verein für Konsumenteninformation v Sofatutor GmbH with regard to the latter). Furthermore, additional regulation may overburden particularly smaller developers and is likely to result in less trials being offered and/or higher subscription prices, both clearly detrimental to consumers' interests.

evidence_quotes.quote: reminders sent before a subscription is automatically renewed or a free trial is converted into a paid subscription,
reminders should only be optional and not the default if still considered necessary.

paragraphs.text: With regards to **reminders** sent before a subscription is automatically renewed or a free trial is converted into a paid subscription, the same considerations apply. As explained, consumers consciously decide to enter into the contract under the transparently presented conditions. Still sending reminders seems superfluous and even excessive and annoying in view of shorter subscription periods. Additionally, it is already the market standard to inform users about the duration of their subscriptions not only before or immediately after they sign a contract. Typically, traders have a section for user account settings in their user interface which allows users to get information about their subscriptions and in just a few steps cancel or change them. This way, consumers can manage their subscriptions without being constantly nagged about ongoing subscriptions from various traders through multiple notifications. Against this background, reminders should only be optional and not the default if still considered necessary.
³⁹https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=278247&pageIndex=0&doclang=EN&mode=lst&dir=&occ=first∂=1&cid=157764

evidence_quotes.quote: "cancellation button"
The high level of consumer safety, information, and cancellation functionality provided by existing means should not be undermined or ignored.

paragraphs.text: In view of this market standard, it is important to also keep the debate on the introduction of a so-called "cancellation button" evidence-based with regards to a relevant consumer need and not to the detriment of existing, customer-friendly solutions. The high level of consumer safety, information, and cancellation functionality provided by existing means should not be undermined or ignored. A new regulation should at least leave enough room for different solutions with the same result and factor in the different types of contracts, products and UI surfaces that exist.

evidence_quotes.quote: to prevent abuse, it is important for traders to be able to clearly identify/verify the consumer requesting a cancellation and to ensure that the request is genuine and originates from the actual subscriber to the service.
In such a scenario, they can also easily use the above-mentioned termination means which are readily available in the user account settings which are very familiar to the user as well.

paragraphs.text: It should also take into account that, to prevent abuse, it is important for traders to be able to clearly identify/verify the consumer requesting a cancellation and to ensure that the request is genuine and originates from the actual subscriber to the service. For digital services, consumers generally have their credentials at hand and do not see authentication as a barrier, but rather a necessary measure to ensure account integrity and prevent abuse. For many services, users would need to access their account to gather the necessary information needed for a safe termination anyway. In such a scenario, they can also easily use the above-mentioned termination means which are readily available in the user account settings which are very familiar to the user as well.

evidence_quotes.quote: Specific regulations on the use of chatbots in this context therefore seem superfluous and unnecessarily limiting companies' flexibility.
However, we would welcome additional guidance on how their use can comply with existing regulations.

paragraphs.text: In this context, we would also like to clarify that from our perspective, the crucial aspect is that users have the opportunity for effective communication related to their contracts, not the specific means used. Specific regulations on the use of chatbots in this context therefore seem superfluous and unnecessarily limiting companies' flexibility. However, we would welcome additional guidance on how their use can comply with existing regulations.

evidence_quotes.quote: focus on transparency

paragraphs.text: With regard to automated contracts, the EC should bear in mind that new provisions would still be based on very limited data. The use of automated contracts in the B2C area is still very limited and the uncertainty mentioned in the Digital Fitness Check as to the kinds of automation tools for consumer contracts that are likely to be rolled out and the extent to which consumers would use them continues to exist. From our perspective, it's currently necessary to be cautious with new regulations in order to avoid hindering new developments. Therefore, we would appreciate a focus on transparency, as we agree that providing clear, concise information about how automation tools work is essential to empower consumers and build trust in these rather new technologies.

evidence_quotes.quote: Finally, we would like to return to free trials and comment on the possibility for traders to require payment details from consumers even where consumers are given a free trial period.

customers already receive transparent and clear information in case subscriptions may automatically continue after the trial period is finished, and have easy methods to cancel if they don't want to continue.

paragraphs.text: Finally, we would like to return to free trials and comment on the possibility for traders to require payment details from consumers even where consumers are given a free trial period. As described above, customers already receive transparent and clear information in case subscriptions may automatically continue after the trial period is finished, and have easy methods to cancel if they don't want to continue. Requiring consumers to provide payment details even where they are given a free trial period ensures service continuity and a seamless user experience where consumers are satisfied with the service. Requesting payment information only at a later date may deteriorate customer experience. It also misinforms as to the character of a "trial" and misleadingly creates the impression of a non-paid service. Additionally, receiving payment details is an abuse prevention mechanism and can often help ensure that users can not open unlimited free trials with different accounts and with no intention to ever pay for provided services. Since free trials often carry a cost to traders / developers to provide to users (e.g. through content acquisition costs and payments to third parties), there is a

evidence_quotes.quote: automatically continue their subscription after the trial has ended.

paragraphs.text: need to balance traders' ability to prevent abuse with the user's ability to acquire a free trial. Traders are also much more likely to offer free trials – which are great for consumers – where there is an easy and seamless way for satisfied consumers to automatically continue their subscription after the trial has ended. Without this easy continuation from a free trial to a paid subscription, the costs to developers of providing free trials may begin to outweigh the benefits, and developers may stop offering them.
Given the extensive digital regulation at both the EU and national levels, we welcome simplification measures.

Between 2019 and 2024 alone, the European Commission passed over 70 laws relating to digital business. The sheer volume of regulations across various laws does not just result in high implementation efforts for businesses. The continuous adjustments and expansions of the legal framework, along with overlaps and unclear relationships between regulations, lead to a high degree of legal uncertainty, which is also reflected in a lack of enforcement. We call for clear and simplified sets of rules that ensure compliance without hurting competitiveness and allows for effective and consistent legal enforcement.
To that end, we encourage the Commission to be guided by three core principles.

First, a smarter, risk-based approach to simplification directly enhances digital fairness for consumers. Focusing compliance efforts on genuine, high-impact harms—rather than applying a one-size-fits-all approach—frees up resources to develop the innovative and intuitive products consumers want. This moves beyond a purely compliance-driven culture to one that improves the online experience and fosters the creation of better, safer services.

Second, simplification must be a principle of good regulation from the outset, not an afterthought. We welcome its inclusion at this early stage of the work on a potential Digital Fairness Act. The Commission should ensure a coherent development of its legislative agenda, in particular by aligning the DFA with the upcoming Digital Simplification Omnibus. This prevents new initiatives from overlapping or contradicting existing ones, ensuring future laws are clear and effective.

Finally, these simplification efforts must be holistic. While supporting small and medium-sized enterprises is vital, simplification cannot be limited to one segment of the economy. The digital world is an interconnected ecosystem; friction at any point in the value chain creates drag for all, from startups to large enterprises. The objective should not be a two-tiered system, but a trust-based framework that gives citizens confidence that their rights are consistently upheld across the Union, while allowing businesses to rely on clear, coherent, and predictable rules.
Differing interpretations and enforcement measures by national authorities and courts run counter to the goal of a harmonized digital single market and lead to significant legal
uncertainty. Companies operating in multiple member states face high administrative burdens and legal costs. This creates unfair conditions.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns (regulated by UCPD, GDPR and DSA)
recommender system transparency (regulated by DSA, CRD, UCPD and P2B, whereby particularly the latter seems superfluous)

paragraphs.text: Many regulations seem to overlap or their boundaries and interplay with other regulations are unclear, which also leads to different enforcement authorities with different conditions and mechanisms having jurisdiction. This is particularly true with regard to topics which shall be addressed by the DFA, including dark patterns (regulated by UCPD, GDPR and DSA) and recommender system transparency (regulated by DSA, CRD, UCPD and P2B, whereby particularly the latter seems superfluous). The overlap leads to confusion and increased efforts, particularly for companies operating across borders.
In addition to these general simplification measures, we believe that specific adjustments to individual regulations are also necessary. These include:
Reducing consumer information:

evidence_quotes.quote: in case of subscriptions, the duration of contract and conditions for terminating

paragraphs.text: We believe that consumer information requirements can be reduced in case of repetitive transactions with the same provider or those concluded via AI assistants. Specifically for account-based transactions where the consumer enters into a framework agreement covering future transactions upon creation of an account, the repetition of most of the information set out in Article 6 CRD seems superfluous as they apply likewise to all transactions. Not displaying this type of information increases the attention on the information essential for the consumer when making another transaction: The main characteristics of the goods or services and the price (and, in case of subscriptions, the duration of contract and conditions for terminating). The repetition of all other information is unnecessary because the consumer knows these are laid down in the framework agreement.

evidence_quotes.quote: The requirement in the CRD to provide a phone number and email for pre-contractual information is outdated.
real-time chat support is more aligned with what consumers expect and prefer.

paragraphs.text: The requirement in the CRD to provide a phone number and email for pre-contractual information is outdated. Today, real-time chat support is more aligned with what consumers expect and prefer.

evidence_quotes.quote: the requirement to provide a withdrawal form for e-commerce purchases seems outdated.

paragraphs.text: Likewise, the requirement to provide a withdrawal form for e-commerce purchases seems outdated.
