It does not reflect consumer expectations in digital environments nor consumer's market practice (e.g., simply returning goods without declaring a withdrawal first, using available means in their account or contacting customer support via electronic means).

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers may consume or "binge" the most valuable content they are interested in within the right of withdrawal period
and would then only incur the compensation obligation of a very small amount if calculated on a pro-rata basis from the monthly/annual/etc. price of the service
the only way to mitigate these risks would be to introduce a rule that access to a digital media subscription service will only be granted 14 days after

paragraphs.text: Rebalance right of withdrawal for digital services: For services that cannot be provided completely within a period shorter than 14 days, consumers may consume or "binge" the most valuable content they are interested in within the right of withdrawal period (e.g., over a weekend) and would then only incur the compensation obligation of a very small amount if calculated on a pro-rata basis from the monthly/annual/etc. price of the service (which may be way more costly to calculate and provide than issuing a full refund). As of now, the only way to mitigate these risks would be to introduce a rule that access to a digital media subscription service will only be granted 14 days after

evidence_quotes.quote: We would therefore welcome that the right of withdrawal for digital services can be excluded under similar circumstances as the right of withdrawal for digital content, at least with regard to digital services providing access to digital content.

paragraphs.text: purchase. However, this would neither be in the interest of the consumer nor the business. This burden seems particularly heavy when it is borne solely out of uncertainty, because an offer cannot be clearly classified as a digital service or digital content due to a lack of sufficient guidance, and in case of doubt, it must be assumed to be a digital service. We would therefore welcome that the right of withdrawal for digital services can be excluded under similar circumstances as the right of withdrawal for digital content, at least with regard to digital services providing access to digital content.

evidence_quotes.quote: Effective age assurance is a critical building block for online child safety.

paragraphs.text: Effective age assurance is a critical building block for online child safety. That said, we urge the Commission not to add a new layer of age assurance requirements to platforms already regulated under the DSA.

section_path: Age assurance is appropriate for many online service providers

paragraphs.text: We support the risk-based approach championed by the EU in the Article 28 DSA Guidelines and the understanding that different age assurance approaches and levels of assurance are appropriate in different circumstances. Age estimation models, for example, can increase online protection while minimising collection of potentially sensitive data from users and can provide proportionate, highly effective age assurance for services that do not pose a high risk.

section_path: Age assurance is appropriate for many online service providers

paragraphs.text: For many years, Google has deployed age assurance methods on our products and services, enabling us to deliver built-in digital protections for kids, teens and parents. Our current first party age estimation model is effective, accurate and proportionate to the risks our services pose, as well as meeting our regulatory needs across the EU.

section_path: Age assurance is appropriate for many online service providers

evidence_quotes.quote: Stricter age verification

paragraphs.text: Stricter age verification, by contrast, is more appropriate where the service poses a high risk to minors — if, say, it is intended primarily for an adult audience (e.g. pornography) — and the developer needs increased assurance of the user's age before permitting access. Cumbersome ID checks across the internet would be detrimental to the consumer experience, raise serious privacy and security implications, and would be a disproportionate measure, divorced from the current EU risk-based approach.

section_path: Age verification is only appropriate for high-risk providers

evidence_quotes.quote: asking users to provide ID or credit card details to verify their age

paragraphs.text: Applying methods for establishing the age of the users that are disproportionate can discourage users from accessing information and key services: for example, asking users to provide ID or credit card details to verify their age in order to check recommendations for restaurants would discourage them from accessing this information in the first place or displace them to less secure services.

section_path: Age verification is only appropriate for high-risk providers

evidence_quotes.quote: Methods for establishing the age of the user also need to be inclusive and take into account the very diverse set of users that make use of online services.
Not all users have access to credit cards or have government-issued IDs.

paragraphs.text: Methods for establishing the age of the user also need to be inclusive and take into account the very diverse set of users that make use of online services. Not all users have access to credit cards or have government-issued IDs. Furthermore, individuals from marginalized or persecuted

section_path: Age verification is only appropriate for high-risk providers

paragraphs.text: groups may lack the trust in the system and be unable or unwilling to share this information, further compounding their disadvantage. Hard identifiers may also pose challenges for children as they are less likely to possess ID-based verification.

section_path: Age verification is only appropriate for high-risk providers

evidence_quotes.quote: age assurance solely at an app store level is a flawed approach

paragraphs.text: As a final remark we note that age assurance solely at an app store level is a flawed approach because it places the responsibility at the wrong point. It is like checking a customer's ID at the entrance of a mall but not at the liquor store or adult movie theater inside. The real risks and content are within the app or the website, not just at the point of download. In addition, such an approach would create privacy and security risks by forcing collection of age data from all app store users, regardless of what apps they use. It is also easily circumvented via web browsers or shared devices and it disregards pre-installed or side-loaded apps. Instead, we believe that online child safety is a shared responsibility, and every player should be required to play their part.

section_path: Age verification is only appropriate for high-risk providers

evidence_quotes.quote: disproportionate costs that must be passed on to consumers.

paragraphs.text: There is no necessity to place traders, including small and medium sized businesses, under a presumption of acting in a non-compliant manner by reversing the burden of proof. Establishing additional requirements for businesses to demonstrate compliance with consumer laws requires additional efforts and ultimately results in disproportionate costs that must be passed on to consumers. It must also be taken into account that the vast majority of consumer law requirements relate to mandatory information that has to be disclosed to consumers as well as other aspects that are transparent throughout the consumer journey, and hence readily available, to consumers.

evidence_quotes.quote: targeting particular consumer segments

paragraphs.text: The existing framework for consumer protection in the EU, centered on the "average consumer" standard, is a flexible, effective and future-proof benchmark. This standard, consistently interpreted by the European Court of Justice for over two decades, considers social, cultural, and linguistic factors, providing a high level of protection while balancing free trade. The framework also includes additional benchmarks for specific situations, such as the "average member of the group" (e.g., in Article 5(2)(b), (3) UCPD) for commercial practices targeting particular consumer segments and the "vulnerable" consumer for those with mental or physical infirmities, age, or credulity (e.g., in Article 5(3) UCPD, recital 62 DSA, recital 75 GDPR). These open-ended concepts allow for a flexible approach to consumer protection without compromising legal certainty for businesses.

evidence_quotes.quote: account for every consumer's individual characteristics and personal circumstances.

paragraphs.text: Introducing new benchmarks or factors to this established framework could undermine legal certainty for businesses, making it difficult for them to account for every consumer's individual characteristics and personal circumstances. Such a change could ultimately hamper consumer protection, as it's impossible to consider the unique and ever-changing characteristics of every single consumer. A more effective way to enhance consumer protection is through other means, such as consumer information and education campaigns, which empower consumers to make informed decisions.
Recent updates to consumer laws, particularly the EU New Deal for Consumers and the DSA, have introduced new obligations (e.g., with regard to ranking of information, the protection of

evidence_quotes.quote: Price Indication Directive

paragraphs.text: minors, price marketing and trader transparency). However, it's currently too early to assess whether further changes are necessary. Harmonizing consumer protection laws across the EU remains a key challenge, with significant deviations still existing between Member States in areas like warranty periods and the applicability of the Price Indication Directive.

evidence_quotes.quote: An explicit "fairness by design" obligation is not necessary and would not offer any added value for consumer protection, since the current consumer protection laws already require us to consider a fair design from the outset.

paragraphs.text: An explicit "fairness by design" obligation is not necessary and would not offer any added value for consumer protection, since the current consumer protection laws already require us to consider a fair design from the outset. There are already various regulations in place that:

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Specify the "fairness by design" principle, such as the numerous pre- and post-contractual information obligations and the prohibitions on using dark patterns.

evidence_quotes.quote: "fairness by design"

paragraphs.text: Already stipulate the "fairness by design" principle, albeit in a negative way. The primary example here is Article 5(1) UCPD, which states, "Unfair commercial practices shall be prohibited." Another example in the privacy field is Article 5(1)(a) GDPR that requires personal data to be processed "fairly" in relation to the data subject.

evidence_quotes.quote: ambiguous "fairness by design" obligation

paragraphs.text: An additional, positively formulated catch-all clause to ensure a fair online environment offers no added value in this regard, as the same result can be achieved by specifying the existing regulations. If the legislator sees a need to regulate specific cases in a certain way, this should be addressed through specific regulations where they do not yet exist, instead of adding an ambiguous "fairness by design" obligation.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
